title: TOTP secret stored in plaintext despite comment claiming encryption at rest
description: Schema comment on `totpSecret` field says `// Encrypted TOTP secret (stored encrypted at rest)`, but the codebase has no encryption helper — `lib/crypto.ts` only contains `generateSecureToken()` (random token generation, not encryption). All 7 2FA API routes (pages/api/auth/2fa/* and pages/api/users/[id]/2fa/*) read/write `totpSecret` as raw base32. A DB dump or SQL injection yields all TOTP seeds; an attacker can then compute valid 6-digit codes for any user. Same problem applies to `webAuthnCreds` (line 74) which stores JSON of credential data. The `secondFactorPending` JSON also contains the pending TOTP secret in plaintext. Either implement envelope encryption (libsodium / KMS) or remove the misleading comment. Also: `webAuthnCreds` (JSON column) is dead — the actual `WebAuthnCredential` table (line 173) is used by the webauthn code, so the JSON column should be dropped to avoid confusion.
suggested_fix: Implement AES-256-GCM encryption of TOTP secrets using a key derived from a secret stored in `process.env` (e.g. `TOTP_ENCRYPTION_KEY`). Add `lib/crypto/secrets.ts` with `encryptSecret()` / `decryptSecret()` helpers. Encrypt at write in `pages/api/users/[id]/2fa/setup.ts` and `webauthn-register-finish.ts` paths; decrypt at read in `verify.ts` and `auth.ts` `authorize()`. Drop the unused `webAuthnCreds` JSON column in a follow-up migration.

title: bcrypt-only password validation ignores passwordMigrationRequired edge case
description: `validatePassword()` short-circuits to `false` when `passwordMigrationRequired` is true, but this means SCrypt-migrated users (the entire pre-existing user base) are completely locked out — they cannot log in even with their correct old password. The comment on line 49 says `must reset password first` but no migration flow exists. A user with `passwordMigrationRequired: true` has no way to recover: there is no `POST /api/auth/forgot-password` or `/reset-password` route in `pages/api/auth/`. The README and AGENTS.md do not document this either. This is a data-loss / account-lockout issue for the entire pre-migration user base, not a security bug per se, but it is a critical UX/correctness defect that masquerades as a security feature.
suggested_fix: Add a password-reset flow: `pages/api/auth/forgot-password.ts` (generates a one-time token, emails a link) and `pages/api/auth/reset-password.ts` (consumes the token, sets a new bcrypt-hashed password, clears `passwordMigrationRequired`). Until implemented, document this clearly in the admin onboarding doc OR run a one-time migration script that hashes all scrypt passwords with bcrypt before the cutover.

title: Wrong import: getServerSession from 'next-auth' instead of 'next-auth/next'
description: Both `pages/api/auth/2fa/setup.ts` (line 2) and `pages/api/auth/2fa/verify.ts` (line 2) import `getServerSession` from `'next-auth'`. In NextAuth.js v5+ (this project uses v5 beta per AGENTS.md), the correct import for API routes / server-side contexts is `'next-auth/next'`. The `next-auth` root package re-exports a client-safe variant that does not read cookies in the API route context, so `getServerSession()` will return `null` and the handler will always 401 — every 2FA setup call from the post-may-10 2fa UI is broken. The sibling `pages/api/auth/ldap.ts` (line 7) uses the correct `'next-auth/next'` import, confirming this is a bug, not a convention choice. The test file `__tests__/api/auth/2fa.test.ts` mocks `@/lib/auth` not `next-auth/next`, so the bug does not surface in unit tests — only at runtime.
suggested_fix: Change `import { getServerSession } from 'next-auth'` to `import { getServerSession } from 'next-auth/next'` in both files. Also audit the other 2FA routes (pages/api/users/[id]/2fa/*) which use the same bad import on lines 3, 8, 9 — they likely have the same runtime bug. Add a vitest integration test that hits the route via `createMocks` without mocking next-auth, to catch this class of issue.

title: TOTP setup endpoint persists secret without authentication gate against the user
description: The action-based `pages/api/auth/2fa/setup.ts` (auth/2fa/, not users/[id]/2fa/) accepts `{action: 'generate'}` from ANY authenticated session, but does not bind the secret to a specific user via path param — it uses `session.user.id` directly. However, the endpoint also accepts `{action: 'enable'}` (line 31) and `{action: 'disable'}` (line 49) using the same path. This means a CSRF or session-fixation attacker who is authenticated as user A could overwrite user B's TOTP secret by hitting this route... but the route uses `session.user.id` not a path param, so the attack is limited to the attacker's own account. Real issue: the `generate` action at line 23 OVERWRITES `totpSecret` and `backupCodes` in the DB BEFORE the user has verified they can produce a valid TOTP code. If the secret write succeeds but the QR scan fails or the user abandons the flow, the user is left with a `totpSecret` set but `totpEnabled=false`. Subsequent login attempts in `lib/auth.ts` `authorize()` (line 76-90) do NOT check `totpEnabled` — they only gate on `passwordHash` and `passwordMigrationRequired`. So login is unaffected, but if a different code path later flips `totpEnabled` to `true` without verification, the user is locked out. More importantly, the `generate` action clears existing `backupCodes` on every call, allowing a DoS where a victim user loses their backup codes.
suggested_fix: Move the `generate` action to a transaction that only commits the secret if verification succeeds, OR rename `totpSecret` to `totpSecretPending` and only promote it to `totpSecret` on successful verify (as the user-scope `pages/api/users/[id]/2fa/setup.ts` does correctly via `secondFactorPending`). Block `generate` from running if 2FA is already enabled and not in an explicit re-enrollment flow. Add a check: refuse `generate` if a pending setup exists <10 min old, to prevent thrashing the QR code.

title: Backup code consumption: linear scan, no constant-time compare, and the 'USED' sentinel is a collision risk
description: `verify.ts` line 39-41: `const idx = hashedCodes.findIndex(h => h !== 'USED' && verifyBackupCode(backupCode, [h]))` calls `verifyBackupCode` per code with a single-element array — this triggers an O(n) re-hash on every iteration, and `verifyBackupCode` uses `hashedCodes.includes(normalized)` which is not constant-time. SHA-256 of a 10-char code has very low entropy (~50 bits given the 32-char alphabet), so a timing attack is theoretically feasible. More serious: the 'USED' sentinel collision risk. A code whose SHA-256 hashes to the literal hex string `USED` (e.g. `c79a1d...` — wait, 'USED' is 4 chars, SHA-256 is 64 hex chars, so a 64-char hash cannot equal 'USED'). Not a collision, but the sentinel is fragile: any future code that treats `user.backupCodes` as an opaque JSON without re-checking `c.used` will see 'USED' as a valid code. Also, the `verify.ts` `method === 'backup'` branch (line 37) does NOT check that `totpEnabled` is true — a user with 2FA disabled can still burn a backup code if one exists in the DB. The branch should be guarded by `if (!user.totpEnabled)` matching the totp branch (line 24).
suggested_fix: Use a `used: boolean` field on each backup-code object (as the user-scope `disable.ts` already does on line 61) and filter on `!c.used` directly, removing the 'USED' sentinel. Switch `verifyBackupCode` to `crypto.timingSafeEqual` after length check. Add `if (!user.totpEnabled) return 400 'TOTP not enabled'` to the backup branch in `pages/api/auth/2fa/verify.ts`.

title: Backup code generation has modulo bias
description: `generateBackupCodes()` uses `b % CODE_CHARS.length` (line 12) on a uniformly random byte. Because 32-byte output is mapped to a 32-character alphabet (256 mod 32 == 0, so no bias), the bias is technically zero for this specific combination. However, if the alphabet length ever changes (e.g. dropping lookalikes `0/O/1/I/l` to 27 chars), bias re-introduces silently. The bigger issue: backup codes are 10 characters from a 32-char alphabet, giving log2(32^10) = 50 bits of entropy. NIST SP 800-63B recommends ≥64 bits for one-time recovery codes. A 50-bit code is brute-forceable in days on a single GPU if the hash is leaked (and the hash is stored in plaintext JSON in the `backupCodes` column). The hash function (line 28) uses plain SHA-256 with no salt and no HMAC, so a pre-computed rainbow table of `sha256(plaincode)` for 10-char codes from this 32-char alphabet is fully tractable (~10^15 hashes, GPU-buildable in hours).
suggested_fix: Generate codes as `crypto.randomBytes(20).toString('base32')` (or use `crypto.randomInt(0, CODE_CHARS.length)` to avoid the bias footgun), giving 20*8=160 bits of entropy. Or expand to 12-14 chars to hit 64 bits. Switch the storage hash to `crypto.scrypt` or `bcrypt.hash(code, 10)` instead of raw SHA-256 to defeat rainbow tables. Add a unique salt per backup code (or HMAC with a server pepper) so identical codes hash differently across users.

title: JWT callback queries the DB on every token refresh — no caching, race window for privilege changes
description: In the `jwt` callback (line 117-125), when `user` is present (initial sign-in), it re-queries `prisma.user.findUnique` to read `isSystemAdmin` and `passwordMigrationRequired`. This is fine at sign-in, but on every subsequent token refresh (every ~hour, default `maxAge`), the same data is already on the token — yet the callback does not refresh from DB, which is good (avoids DB load) but bad for privilege-revocation latency: demoting a system admin or marking a user for password migration only takes effect at the next JWT re-issue (max 1 hour, or until forced re-auth). A more serious bug: when `user` is undefined (token refresh path), the `if (user)` block is skipped, so `token.isSystemAdmin` and `token.passwordMigrationRequired` are never re-fetched — the code relies on the values already being on the token from sign-in. But the `signIn` event handler / `jwt` callback for OAuth providers (Google/GitHub) on subsequent visits: if the user is found via the OAuth provider and the OAuth provider returns a `user` object, this branch re-runs. So the design is OK, but there is no way to force a DB refresh (e.g. when an admin demotes another admin) without manually revoking the session. There is also no `events.signOut` or `events.session` handler to clear the `isSystemAdmin` flag on sign-out (cosmetic, but JWT strategy means tokens are stateless and the cookie just expires).
suggested_fix: Add a `session.user.lastCheckedAt` and re-fetch from DB if the check is older than N minutes (e.g. 5). OR add a `users.jwtVersion` int column, increment it on role/privilege changes, and re-check the version in the `jwt` callback — if mismatched, return `null` to force re-auth. Add `events.signOut` to log the sign-out and clear any in-memory caches.

title: Google/GitHub OAuth providers initialized with empty strings when env vars missing
description: Lines 57-64 initialize Google and GitHub providers with `process.env.GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID ?? ''`. If the env var is empty, the OAuth library gets `clientId: ''` and `clientSecret: ''`. NextAuth will then render the sign-in page with provider buttons, but clicking them will return an error like `OAuthSignin` from the provider — but only after a redirect round-trip, leaking the existence of the misconfiguration. More importantly, the providers array always includes Google and GitHub even if they are not configured, which means the `[...nextauth].ts` catch-all will route `/api/auth/signin/google` and `/api/auth/signin/github` requests to providers that cannot complete. The auth-guard test (`__tests__/api/auth-guard.test.ts`) allows public access to `/api/auth/test123` (the catch-all), so this is not a security hole, but a UX bug.
suggested_fix: Conditionally include the providers: `...(process.env.GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID ? [GoogleProvider({...})] : [])`. Add a startup check that logs a warning if either provider is enabled without both client ID and secret set. Document in `.env.example` that empty OAuth vars mean the provider should be removed entirely, not just left blank.

title: Credentials provider case-sensitive email lookup enables account enumeration via timing
description: `authorize()` line 76: `prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { email: credentials.email as string }})`. Prisma's `findUnique` is case-sensitive (Postgres default collation). An attacker can probe whether `[email protected]` exists vs `[email protected]` by measuring response time AND by the explicit `return null` vs `if (!user || !user.passwordHash)` (which silently returns the same null but for different reasons). The current `validatePassword` short-circuit on `passwordMigrationRequired` is a separate timing oracle. The response on line 81 (`!user || !user.passwordHash`) collapses two cases, but the `passwordMigrationRequired` check (line 85) is a separate path. Fix: do a dummy bcrypt.compare against a fixed string on the not-found path to equalize timing.
suggested_fix: Add a constant-time equalization: if `!user || !user.passwordHash`, run `await bcrypt.compare(credentials.password, '$2a$10$invalidsaltinvalidsaltinO9vXJqYH6z5k2H9g5xvX5Z5Z5Z5Z5Z')` and return null. This matches the timing of a real bcrypt compare. Additionally, lowercase the email before lookup: `where: { email: credentials.email.toLowerCase() }` and ensure the DB stores normalized lowercase emails (add a CHECK constraint or a pre-write hook).

title: API key lookup is O(n) full-table scan and no rate limiting
description: `validateApiKey()` uses `prisma.user.findFirst({ where: { apiKey } })` (line 31). Without an index on `User.apiKey` (line 79 of schema.prisma), this is a full-table scan — slow at scale and exploitable as a DoS amplifier (each call scans all users). Also, the `apiKey` column has no `@unique` constraint, so two users could (theoretically) share the same key. The `User.apiKey` field is a single String, not an index — no incremental rotation, no per-key createdAt/expiry. There is no rate limiting on the API key auth path; brute-forcing a 32-char token over HTTP would take centuries, but a stolen key is forever-valid with no audit trail of which endpoint was called. The function returns `{ userId, error }` and is called from `lib/api/v3/*` endpoints, but the caller is responsible for emitting audit events — no `recordSecurityEvent` call here, so a leaked key has no detection signal.
suggested_fix: Add `@@index([apiKey])` to the User model. Consider migrating to a `PersonalAccessToken` table with `{ id, userId, hashedToken (sha256), name, scopes[], lastUsedAt, expiresAt }` — store only the hash, never the plaintext, and rotate via token-versioning. Wrap the lookup in a rate limiter (Upstash, already in the stack). Emit a `API_KEY_USED` security event on every auth (or batch every N minutes for hot paths).

title: Challenge deletion race condition and silently-allowed Redis unavailability
description: `consumeChallenge()` uses `getdel` to read-and-delete the challenge in one round-trip (line 93), which is good — but if Redis is unconfigured, `getRedis()` returns null and `consumeChallenge` returns null, which the verify functions treat as `verified: false` and emit a `WEBAUTHN_CHALLENGE_MISS` event. So Redis-down => 2FA is hard-disabled. But the `persistChallenge` function (line 61-75) ALSO tolerates `r` being null and STILL returns the encoded challenge to the client — so the client will try to register with a challenge that was never stored. The verify step then fails and the user sees a confusing error. Worse: in a partial Redis outage where the SET succeeds but the GETDEL fails (network blip), the challenge remains in Redis with a 5-min TTL, and a second registration attempt within that window will fail with a stale challenge. There is also no per-device fingerprinting: a single challenge can be used for any `response.id` — but verifyRegistrationResponse checks the credential ID against the challenge, so this is fine in practice.
suggested_fix: Make `persistChallenge` fail loudly (throw or return a `redisAvailable: false` flag) when Redis is null, so the API route can surface a 503 to the client. On verify failure, if the challenge lookup fails AND a `lastPersistedAt` shows a recent write, the challenge is in a 'stuck' state — add a background job to GC stale challenges, or document a manual `redis-cli DEL webauthn:challenge:*` recovery command. Log the Redis-unconfigured case at startup, not per-request, to avoid log spam.

title: verifyAuthentication response.id used directly, not from DB credential
description: `verifyAuthentication()` (line 230) passes `id: response.id` to the `credential` argument of `verifyAuthenticationResponse`. This is the credential ID supplied by the client in the response, not the one stored in the DB. The caller is supposed to look up the credential by userId+credentialId before calling verify, but `verifyAuthentication` itself does not do that lookup — it accepts the credential's public key + counter as a separate argument, leaving the matching of `response.id` to the stored credential to the caller. If the caller passes the wrong credential, the signature will be verified against a mismatched public key and the verify will fail safely — but the response.id is taken at face value for logging/auditing. There is no check that `response.id` actually matches the credential we loaded. A future caller that reorders arguments could silently accept a wrong-credential signature.
suggested_fix: Have `verifyAuthentication` accept the full `Credential` row from Prisma and verify internally: `if (response.id !== credential.credentialId) return { verified: false, reason: 'credential_mismatch' }`. Emit a `WEBAUTHN_CREDENTIAL_MISMATCH` security event. This makes the API misuse-resistant.

title: Backup code disable path uses plaintext-equality check on plainCode
description: `disable.ts` line 61-62: `JSON.parse(user.backupCodes)` then `c.plainCode?.toLowerCase() === backupCode.toLowerCase()`. The `backupCodes` column in the DB stores the JSON from `pages/api/users/[id]/2fa/setup.ts` line 51-53, which contains `{hashedCode, plainCode, used: false}` — so the `plainCode` field IS the unhashed code. Storing plaintext backup codes in the DB means a DB read yields every backup code for every user in cleartext. The whole point of hashed storage (which the field name `hashedCodes` and the `hashBackupCode` helper in `backup-codes.ts` implies) is defeated. Compare: the `pages/api/auth/2fa/verify.ts` path (line 38) does the right thing — uses `verifyBackupCode(backupCode, [h])` which checks against the hash, not the plain. So there are TWO inconsistent code paths for the same operation.
suggested_fix: Migrate the disable path to use the hash: store only `{hashedCode, used}` (drop `plainCode`). Then `disable.ts` does `verifyBackupCode(backupCode, [c.hashedCode])` and sets `c.used = true`. Backfill: a one-time script to hash all existing `plainCode` values, then drop the column. This is also required to fix AUTH-6 (the SHA-256 rainbow table risk): once `plainCode` is removed from storage, an attacker who reads the DB column only sees hashes.

title: Migration-blocked user gets identical 401 timing as wrong password — good for enumeration, but error message differs in 2fa setup
description: Line 85-88 returns `null` (silent) when `passwordMigrationRequired` is true, which is good — it does not reveal that the account exists. But the same user will then try to log in via the `/login` UI, and the UI will probably display a generic 'Invalid credentials' message. There is no path for the user to discover WHY they cannot log in (no `/api/auth/status` endpoint, no email with reset link). The migration flow as designed assumes an admin will manually reset the password, but no admin UI for this exists. Coverage gap: there is no test that verifies the password-migration block actually returns the same response shape and timing as a wrong-password attempt. The comment on line 86 says `// Don't reveal whether the account exists` but this is only true at the response level — at the audit-log level, a `AUTH_LOGIN_FAILED` event with `passwordMigrationRequired: true` is distinguishable.
suggested_fix: Add an integration test that times the wrong-password and migration-blocked paths and asserts they are within ±5ms. Add a `GET /api/auth/login-status?email=...` (public, no PII) endpoint that returns a generic `ok` plus, ONLY if the account is migration-blocked, a hint like `'Please contact your administrator to reset your password'`. Or implement the full reset-password flow (see AUTH-2).

title: Critical 2FA endpoints are entirely untested at the unit level
description: The test file `__tests__/api/auth/2fa.test.ts` (38 lines) covers only two cases: `setup.ts` and `verify.ts` returning 401 when no session. Neither the TOTP secret generation, the TOTP code verification, the backup-code consumption logic, the 2FA disable flow, the WebAuthn register-options flow, nor the WebAuthn register-finish flow has a unit test. More critically, the test file imports `setupHandler from '@/pages/api/auth/2fa/setup'` and `verifyHandler from '@/pages/api/auth/2fa/verify'` — but the actual project uses `pages/api/users/[id]/2fa/setup.ts` and `verify.ts` (different path, per-route-user-scope). This means the tests target an older OR duplicate set of routes that may not be wired up to the UI. The `__tests__/lib/2fa/webauthn.test.ts` (33 lines) is a thin mock-only test that never calls the actual `lib/2fa/webauthn.ts` code (it mocks `@simplewebauthn/server` and tests the mock's return shape). The `__tests__/lib/oauth.unit.test.ts` (272 lines) is even worse: lines 18-72 test a `generateStateToken` / `verifyStateToken` function that is NOT exported from anywhere in `lib/auth.ts` — it is a test-local helper that re-implements the state-token pattern in isolation, so the test never actually exercises the real auth code path. These tests are providing false confidence in CI.
suggested_fix: Rewrite `__tests__/api/auth/2fa.test.ts` to target the actual `pages/api/users/[id]/2fa/setup.ts`, `verify.ts`, `disable.ts`, `index.ts`, and `webauthn-register-finish.ts` endpoints. Add at least one test per code path: setup→verify happy path, wrong-code rejection, expired-pending rejection, backup-code consumption, disable-with-password-and-totp, disable-with-password-and-backup-code, disable-without-2fa-error, webauthn registration with mocked simplewebauthn. For the OAuth tests, import and exercise the actual `jwt` and `session` callbacks from `lib/auth.ts` with a mock NextAuth context. For the WebAuthn tests, exercise the real `consumeChallenge` / `persistChallenge` Redis paths with a mocked Redis (in-memory or ioredis-mock).

title: NextAuth v4-style export, project claims v5
description: `pages/api/auth/[...nextauth].ts` is the legacy Pages Router catch-all. It uses `import NextAuth from 'next-auth'` and `export default NextAuth(authOptions)` (line 4). The `lib/auth.ts` also uses `import NextAuth from 'next-auth'` (line 1) and `export default NextAuth(authOptions)` (line 144). The AGENTS.md says the project uses `NextAuth.js v5 (beta)`. NextAuth v5 (`next-auth@5.x`) uses a different API: `import NextAuth from 'next-auth'; export const { auth, handlers, signIn, signOut } = NextAuth({...})` with the catch-all at `app/api/auth/[...nextauth]/route.ts` (App Router). The v4 Pages Router style shown here is the v4 API. If v5 is actually installed, the package's v4-compat shim still works, but the v5 features (edge runtime, improved `auth()` helper, etc.) are not available. This is a maintenance confusion risk, not a security issue, but it ties into the import bug in AUTH-3.
suggested_fix: Verify the installed version: `npm ls next-auth` (do not run; just inspect `package.json` — should be `^5.0.0-beta.x`). If v5 is installed, migrate the catch-all to App Router at `app/api/auth/[...nextauth]/route.ts` and use the v5 handler pattern. If v4 is installed, update AGENTS.md to say v4. Either way, make the version explicit in `package.json` with an exact version range (no caret on beta) to prevent drift.

title: Duplicated type augmentation diverges from lib/auth.ts
description: `types/next-auth.d.ts` augments `Session` with `id, email, name, image, passwordMigrationRequired, isSystemAdmin`. The same augmentation is in `lib/auth.ts` lines 10-21 (declare module 'next-auth'). TypeScript will merge these, so it compiles, but the two declarations will drift — one will add a field and the other won't, and the compile error will be confusing. `lib/auth.ts` also augments the `JWT` interface (lines 23-29) which `types/next-auth.d.ts` does not. Coverage: no test verifies that the session callback actually populates these fields — the oauth.unit.test.ts test is purely on a local mock token, never on a real `getServerSession()` call. So a regression that drops `isSystemAdmin` from the session would not be caught.
suggested_fix: Move the type augmentation to ONE file (either `types/next-auth.d.ts` and remove the inline declare in `lib/auth.ts`, or vice versa). Add a vitest test that imports `authOptions`, calls the `session` callback with a mock token, and asserts the returned session has `isSystemAdmin` and `passwordMigrationRequired` populated correctly. Add a test for the `jwt` callback that asserts `token.isSystemAdmin` and `token.passwordMigrationRequired` are set after a credentials login with a mock Prisma `findUnique`.

title: createBackupCodeHashes builds inverted Map that is never consumed
description: `createBackupCodeHashes` returns `Map<hashedCode, plainCode>` (line 22). The caller `pages/api/users/[id]/2fa/setup.ts` line 50 does `Array.from(hashedCodes.entries()).map(([hashed, plain]) => ({hashedCode: hashed, plainCode: plain, used: false}))` — extracting entries, ignoring the Map structure, and rebuilding objects. The Map is purely an intermediate; the API would be clearer as `hashBackupCodes(codes: string[]): Array<{hashedCode: string; plainCode: string}>` returning the same shape the caller wants. As-is, the Map is allocated, immediately destructured, and discarded — wasted work and a misleading API. Not a security issue, just dead/awkward code.
suggested_fix: Replace `createBackupCodeHashes` with a function that returns `Array<{hashedCode, plainCode}>` directly, or remove it entirely and have `setup.ts` do `codes.map(c => ({hashedCode: hashBackupCode(c), plainCode: c, used: false}))` inline. Update `pages/api/auth/2fa/setup.ts` (the duplicate in the auth/2fa path) to use the same shape for consistency.
